name: Pediatrics: The Child with a Genitourinary Condition

scenarios.question: What is the most common cause of UTI in children?
scenarios.rationale: E. coli (80-90%): gram-negative, ascending from perineum. Risk factors: uncircumcised males, vesicoureteral reflux, poor hygiene. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child with a UTI presents with:
scenarios.rationale: UTI in children: fever (especially <2 years), dysuria, frequency, urgency, abdominal/flank pain, enuresis. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is vesicoureteral reflux (VUR)?
options: Backward flow of urine from bladder to ureter
scenarios.rationale: VUR: urine refluxes to ureter/kidney during voiding → recurrent UTI, scarring, hypertension. Grades I-V. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Hypospadias is:
options: Urethral opening on the ventral surface of the penis
Urethral opening on the dorsal surface
scenarios.rationale: Hypospadias: ventral placement of meatus. Often with chordee (ventral curvature). Surgical repair. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Cryptorchidism refers to:
scenarios.rationale: Cryptorchidism: testicle fails to descend into scrotum. Risk of torsion, malignancy if untreated. Orchiopexy by 12-18 months. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is phimosis?
scenarios.rationale: Phimosis: foreskin cannot be retracted over glans. Normal in infants; pathological if causes problems after age 3. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Enuresis is defined as:
options: Involuntary urination after expected age of control
scenarios.rationale: Enuresis: bedwetting after age 5 (nocturnal) or daytime wetting after age 4. Primary vs secondary. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The first-line treatment for primary nocturnal enuresis is:
scenarios.rationale: Bed alarm + positive reinforcement first-line. Medications (desmopressin) for short-term (camp, sleepovers). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the most common renal disorder in children?
scenarios.rationale: Nephrotic syndrome: massive proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia. Minimal change disease most common. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child with nephrotic syndrome presents with:
options: Periorbital edema, generalized edema, foamy urine
scenarios.rationale: Nephrotic: edema (periorbital, abdominal, genital), weight gain, frothy urine (protein), fatigue. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) is:
options: Most common renal malignancy in children
scenarios.rationale: Wilms tumor: most common renal tumor in children. Don't palpate flank (risk of rupture). Surgery + chemo. (Quizlet Peds GU)

scenarios.question: A child with Wilms tumor should NOT have:
scenarios.rationale: Wilms tumor: avoid flank palpation (can rupture/disseminate). Abdominal mass found incidentally. (Quizlet Peds GU)

scenarios.question: Glomerulonephritis in children is often preceded by:
scenarios.rationale: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis: 1-3 weeks after strep infection. Hematuria, edema, hypertension. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Acute glomerulonephritis presents with:
scenarios.rationale: AGN: hematuria (cola-colored urine), periorbital edema, hypertension, decreased urine output. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Hypospadias repair is typically done at:
scenarios.rationale: Hypospadias repair: 6-18 months before child is aware. Also corrects chordee if present. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Undescended testes (cryptorchidism) should be surgically corrected by:
scenarios.rationale: Orchiopexy by 12-18 months to prevent torsion, malignancy, infertility. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Circumcision is contraindicated if:
scenarios.rationale: Circumcision contraindicated with hypospadias, chordee, epispadias — foreskin needed for repair. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Nephrotic syndrome is characterized by:
scenarios.rationale: Nephrotic: 4 features — proteinuria (>3.5g/day), low albumin, edema, high lipids. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Prednisone is used in nephrotic syndrome to:
scenarios.rationale: Nephrotic: steroids (prednisone) induce remission in minimal change disease. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Acute kidney injury in children can result from:
scenarios.rationale: AKI in children: prerenal (dehydration), intrinsic (nephrotoxins, glomerulonephritis), postrenal (obstruction). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child with UTI and fever needs:
options: Prompt antibiotic treatment to prevent scarring
scenarios.rationale: Febrile UTI: treat promptly with antibiotics. Risk of renal scarring, especially with VUR. (Standard NCLEX)
